candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made non-circular and operationalized into a testable criterion for 'strange-loop-permitting' universes? The core unresolved tension is whether 'recursively interpretable' picks out a genuine physical constraint beyond anthropic restatement. This requires defining measurable necessary conditions for self-modeling systems—e.g., depth of stable hierarchical organization, computational capacity, memory, error-correction, and long-lived energy gradients—and showing that these conditions carve out a narrower or different region of parameter space than standard life-permitting or observer-permitting criteria.

Is reflective self-reference actually a distinct fine-tuning target, or does it reduce to generic open-ended complexity under known physics? The conjecture assumes a substantive gap between mere complexity and epistemic loops, but it is unresolved whether self-modeling requires special additional structure or simply emerges once evolution, computation, and long timescales are available. If the latter, the conjecture may add little explanatory content; if the former, we need a theory of what extra structural features make self-reference possible and why constants should be especially sensitive to them.

What is the modal scope of the claim across possible laws, substrates, and forms of cognition? The conjecture is framed using our universe's atoms, stars, chemistry, and biological-style hierarchy, but an open question is whether strange loops could arise in radically different physics or non-chemical substrates. If self-referential observers are possible in many very different lawful worlds, then the alleged narrow viability band may be much wider than suggested; if not, we need principled arguments for why self-reference depends on specific kinds of multiscale stability and feedback rather than merely some alternative route to computation and representation.
